query: reflow vs repaint javascript interview question

competitors.label: Animation performance and frame rate
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for the rendering waterfall, property cost, and why layout-triggering animations create jank.
competitors.winReason: MDN is authoritative on rendering cost, but our answer is tighter for this exact interview question because it pairs the layout-vs-paint distinction with a direct forced-reflow bug example and a more explicit explanation of why geometry changes cascade.
theirStrengths: It clearly explains the rendering waterfall and which CSS properties trigger layout, repaint, or compositing work.
theirGaps: It is broader animation guidance, while our page is more directly shaped around reflow-vs-repaint interview confusion and runtime debugging.

actionableExamples: A common performance bug: write styles, then immediately read layout metrics, repeatedly in a loop.
followUpConfusion: Reflow is usually more expensive because geometry changes can cascade through large parts of the DOM tree.

realWorldUseCases: The page ties layout cost to animation jank and frame-budget pressure, so its real-world use-case framing is at least as strong as ours.
actionableExamples: Our answer is more concrete for this narrow question because it shows repaint-only vs layout-changing updates and then names forced synchronous reflow as a common bug.
followUpConfusion: Our page is sharper on the exact distinction candidates usually mix up: geometry changes cascading through the DOM versus pixel-only updates.

realWorldUseCases: Add one sentence tying reflow spikes to INP or scroll jank in a production trace, not just animation smoothness.

competitors.label: What are some techniques for reducing reflows and repaints?
competitors.selectionReason: This is GreatFrontEnd's direct interview-prep page on reflows and repaints, so it is the closest same-intent interview comparator.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd gives a longer mitigation checklist, but our page is now stronger on the exact interview answer because it pairs a richer production scenario with a clearer explanation of paint-heavy work versus layout work versus composite updates.
theirStrengths: It gives a broad checklist of practical mitigations such as batching DOM changes, using requestAnimationFrame, and avoiding layout thrashing.
theirGaps: It is lighter on the conceptual distinction between layout, repaint, and composite cost.

realWorldUseCases: A filter sidebar and analytics dashboard both update card dimensions on every keystroke, causing repeated layout reads and writes that create visible typing lag.
followUpConfusion: The two most asked stages are: - Reflow (layout): recalculate element size/position. - Repaint: redraw pixels (colors, shadows, visibility) without changing geometry.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd anchors the topic in performance work too, but our page now tells a richer production story that combines layout reads, dashboard jank, and paint-heavy visuals.
actionableExamples: GreatFrontEnd gives a longer mitigation list with document fragments, CSS classes, requestAnimationFrame, will-change, and read/write separation.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the under-the-hood difference between layout and repaint instead of jumping directly to optimization tips.

actionableExamples: Add a short bullet list covering document fragments, CSS class batching, and requestAnimationFrame as mitigation patterns.

competitors.label: Avoid large, complex layouts and layout thrashing
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev is a strong performance reference for forced synchronous layout and layout-thrashing debugging patterns.
competitors.winReason: web.dev is still more detailed on one layout-thrashing pattern, but our page is now better at the broader interview comparison because it explains layout, paint, and composite together and ties them to a concrete dashboard-style jank incident.
theirStrengths: It gives a very concrete read-then-write example and explains exactly why offsetWidth inside a write loop forces repeated layout.
theirGaps: It is more focused on layout thrashing than on the broader reflow-vs-repaint distinction.

realWorldUseCases: A filter sidebar and analytics dashboard both update card dimensions on every keystroke, causing repeated layout reads and writes that create visible typing lag.
followUpConfusion: Paint-only changes can still be expensive when they redraw large backgrounds, shadows, filters, or blur effects.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev frames layout thrashing as a production problem too, but our page is now broader on the kind of UI incident interviewers often ask about: layout jank plus heavy paint work together.
actionableExamples: It walks through a concrete offsetWidth loop, explains the read-write-read-write cycle, and then shows the fixed read-first pattern.
followUpConfusion: web.dev is excellent on why repeated reads and writes force layout, but our page now explains the additional follow-up confusion around repaint-heavy effects and composite-only updates more directly.

actionableExamples: Add a second code example that explicitly batches DOM reads before writes.
